title: The WAEC Grading System Explained: What Your A1, B2, and C4 Actually Mean
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You receive your WAEC result. You see letters and numbers: A1, B2, C5, D7, F9. You know A is good and F is bad, but you are not sure what the numbers mean, how the grades are calculated, or what universities will accept. The WAEC grading system is not the same as the A-B-C system used in some other countries. Understanding it is important because your grades determine your university admission eligibility, your course options, and in some cases, your scholarship opportunities. Here is exactly what each grade means and how they are determined.

The Grade Scale: A1 to F9

WAEC uses a nine-point grading scale. A1 is the highest grade. It represents excellent performance and corresponds to a score of roughly seventy-five percent and above, though the exact cutoff varies by subject and by year depending on the difficulty of the exam. B2 is very good, typically representing a score of seventy to seventy-four percent. B3 is good, representing sixty-five to sixty-nine percent. C4, C5, and C6 are credit grades. C4 is roughly sixty to sixty-four percent. C5 is fifty-five to fifty-nine percent. C6 is fifty to fifty-four percent. These six grades, A1 through C6, are considered credit passes. They are the grades that count toward university admission requirements.

D7 and E8 are passing grades but not credit grades. They indicate that you passed the subject but did not perform well enough to earn a credit. F9 is a fail. It means you did not meet the minimum standard for a pass. For university admission purposes, D7, E8, and F9 do not count toward your five required credits. If you earn a D7 in Mathematics, you have not met the Mathematics requirement, even though you technically passed the subject.

The percentage ranges I have given are approximate. WAEC does not publish exact grade boundaries, and they shift slightly each year based on the performance of all candidates. A difficult exam may have slightly lower boundaries. An easier exam may have slightly higher ones. The process is called norm referencing, and it means your grade depends partly on how you performed relative to other candidates, not just on your absolute score.

How Raw Scores Become Grades

Your WAEC grade is not simply your percentage of correct answers converted directly to a letter. The process involves combining your scores from different papers within the same subject. Most WAEC subjects have multiple components. English Language, for example, has Paper 1 (objective), Paper 2 (theory and essay), and Paper 3 (oral English). Each paper contributes a specific percentage to your total score. The total is then mapped onto the A1 to F9 scale.

This means you cannot afford to neglect any paper. A student who performs excellently on the objective paper but poorly on the theory paper may still end up with a low grade because the theory paper carries significant weight. Similarly, a student who skips the oral English paper will lose those marks entirely, which can drop them from a credit to a pass or from a pass to a fail. You need to perform consistently across all papers within a subject.

The practical papers in science subjects also carry weight. Biology, Chemistry, and Physics each have a practical component that contributes to your final grade. Students sometimes treat the practical as less important than the theory. This is a costly mistake. The practical marks are part of your total, and losing them can drag your grade down significantly.

What Universities Require

Most Nigerian universities require at least five credit passes, including English Language and Mathematics, in no more than two sittings. Five credit passes means five subjects at C6 or higher. The requirement for English and Mathematics is non-negotiable. Even if you have credit passes in eight other subjects, a D7 in English or Mathematics will block your admission.

Beyond the general requirement, specific courses have specific subject requirements. A student applying for Medicine typically needs credit passes in English, Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. A student applying for Law typically needs credit passes in English, Mathematics, Literature in English, and Government or History. A student applying for Accounting typically needs credit passes in English, Mathematics, Economics, and one other commercial subject. The specific requirements vary by university and by course. Always check the current JAMB brochure or the university's admissions page for the exact requirements for your intended course.

Some competitive courses at top universities require grades higher than the minimum C6. A university might state that applicants for Medicine must have at least a B3 in Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. The minimum requirement gets your application considered. The competitive requirement gets you admitted. In a course with many applicants and limited spaces, the university will select candidates with the strongest grades. A student with A1 and B2 grades across their relevant subjects is more competitive than a student with C5 and C6 grades, even though both technically meet the minimum.

Combining Results Across Sittings

WAEC allows you to combine results from two different exam sittings. If you took WAEC in May or June and did not earn a credit in Mathematics, you can retake Mathematics in the November or December private candidate exam. If you earn a credit in the second sitting, you can combine it with your credits from the first sitting to meet the five-credit requirement. Most universities accept combined results, though some competitive programs prefer or require results from a single sitting.

You can also combine WAEC results with NECO results. A credit in WAEC English and a credit in NECO Mathematics count as two of your five required credits. The combination flexibility means that one poor performance does not close all doors. You have options. Use them strategically.

The Bottom Line

The WAEC grading scale runs from A1 (excellent) to F9 (fail). Credits are C6 and above. D7 and E8 are passes but not credits. Universities typically require five credits including English and Mathematics, with specific subject requirements for specific courses. Grades are determined by combining scores across all papers within a subject, so do not neglect any component. You can combine results across two sittings or across WAEC and NECO. Understand the system, know what your target universities require, and plan your preparation accordingly.
